learning_objectives: Apply systematic methods to identify all project stakeholders
Classify stakeholders using power, interest, influence, and impact models

lecture_notes.introduction: Stakeholder management is about understanding who can affect or be affected by the project and proactively managing those relationships.
key_concepts: Stakeholder definition: individuals, groups, or organizations that can affect or be affected by the project
Identification techniques: stakeholder brainstorming, organizational charts, expert judgment
Power-Interest grid: manage closely, keep satisfied, keep informed, monitor
Salience model: power, legitimacy, urgency determine stakeholder salience
Stakeholder register: name, role, interest, influence, attitude, communication needs
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping stakeholders for a hospital IT system implementation using multiple classification models

review.value: Stakeholders with all three attributes (power, legitimacy, urgency) are definitive stakeholders requiring the most attention
Regulators, end users, and community members are often overlooked; failure to identify them causes late-stage opposition

learning_objectives: Assess current and desired stakeholder engagement levels
Develop a stakeholder engagement plan with targeted strategies
Distinguish between awareness levels: unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading

lecture_notes.introduction: Knowing who stakeholders are is not enough. You must actively plan how to move each stakeholder to the engagement level the project needs.
key_concepts: Engagement assessment matrix: current (C) vs. desired (D) for each stakeholder
Engagement levels: unaware, resistant, neutral, supportive, leading
Engagement strategies: inform, consult, involve, collaborate, empower
Stakeholder engagement plan: who, current level, desired level, strategy, responsible party
Timing: engagement needs change throughout the project life cycle
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an engagement assessment matrix for a city infrastructure project with 12 stakeholder groups

review.value: The gap between current and desired engagement shows where effort is needed; large gaps require dedicated strategies
Inform (one-way), Consult (gather input), Involve (work together), Collaborate (partner), Empower (delegate decision-making)

learning_objectives: Negotiate and align stakeholder expectations with project realities
Apply expectation management techniques throughout the project

lecture_notes.introduction: Unmanaged expectations are the leading cause of stakeholder dissatisfaction. Even successful projects fail if expectations were never aligned.
key_concepts: Expectation gap: difference between what stakeholders expect and what the project will deliver
Setting expectations early: scope statement, assumptions, constraints, exclusions
Managing scope creep through stakeholder expectations
Prioritizing conflicting requirements: MoSCoW, weighted scoring, stakeholder power analysis
Underpromise and overdeliver: managing the perception of success
lecture_notes.demonstration: Role-playing a requirements prioritization meeting with three stakeholders holding conflicting priorities

review.value: Document what is IN scope AND what is OUT of scope; explicit exclusions prevent assumptions
Use objective criteria (business value, cost, risk) not politics to resolve conflicts; escalate to the sponsor when needed

learning_objectives: Identify root causes of stakeholder resistance
Apply techniques to convert resistant stakeholders
Manage hostile or obstructive stakeholder behavior

lecture_notes.introduction: Resistance is natural. People resist when they perceive a threat to their status, autonomy, competence, or relationships.
key_concepts: Resistance sources: loss of control, uncertainty, threat to status quo, past negative experiences
SCARF model: Status, Certainty, Autonomy, Relatedness, Fairness as threat triggers
Converting resistance: listen first, acknowledge concerns, find common ground, demonstrate quick wins
Coalition building: using supportive stakeholders to influence resistant ones
When to escalate vs. when to work around resistant stakeholders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis of a failed ERP implementation due to middle management resistance, with recovery plan

review.value: Threats to any SCARF element trigger the same brain response as physical threats; minimize threats and maximize rewards
Early visible successes build credibility and momentum that reduces resistance over time

learning_objectives: Manage stakeholders in multi-project and program environments
Apply stakeholder management to regulated and public-sector projects
Handle stakeholder dynamics in mergers, acquisitions, and transformations
readings: Eskerod & Jepsen, Project Stakeholder Management, Ch. 6-7

lecture_notes.introduction: Complex projects have stakeholder ecosystems, not just stakeholder lists. Understanding the dynamics between stakeholders is as important as managing individual ones.
key_concepts: Stakeholder ecosystems: networks of relationships and influences between stakeholders
Social network analysis: mapping influence pathways between stakeholder groups
Regulatory stakeholders: compliance requirements, public comment periods, approval processes
Political dynamics: organizational politics, hidden agendas, informal power structures
Transformation projects: fear of job loss, culture change resistance, identity threats
lecture_notes.demonstration: Social network analysis of stakeholder relationships in a government IT modernization project
setup_requirements: Social network analysis tool or template

review.value: Identifies key connectors and influencers who may not have formal authority but can sway opinions
Cannot be managed through influence alone; must satisfy formal requirements and approval processes

class_schedule.topic: Monitoring Stakeholder Engagement and Continuous Improvement
learning_objectives: Monitor and measure stakeholder engagement effectiveness
Adapt engagement strategies based on feedback and changing dynamics

lecture_notes.introduction: Stakeholder engagement is not set-and-forget. Relationships evolve, new stakeholders emerge, and engagement strategies must adapt.
key_concepts: Engagement metrics: survey results, meeting attendance, issue resolution speed, feedback quality
Stakeholder sentiment tracking: regular pulse checks on satisfaction and concerns
Reassessment triggers: scope changes, personnel changes, project crises, phase transitions
Stakeholder engagement as a competitive advantage: building organizational relationship capital
Lessons learned: what worked, what did not, key stakeholder insights for future projects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a stakeholder engagement dashboard with leading and lagging indicators

review.value: Proactive meeting attendance, quality of stakeholder input, and speed of response predict future engagement
Major project changes require stakeholder re-analysis; the stakeholder landscape at phase 3 differs from phase 1

quizzes.topic: Stakeholder Identification, Analysis, and Engagement Planning

questions.question: A new CRM system project has the following stakeholders: CEO (supportive, high power), Sales VP (resistant, high power), IT Director (supportive, medium power), Customer Service Manager (neutral, low power). Using the engagement assessment matrix, identify the priority stakeholder and recommend a specific engagement strategy.
questions.answer: The Sales VP is the priority stakeholder because they have high power and are resistant. The gap between current (resistant) and desired (supportive) is the largest among high-power stakeholders. Strategy: (1) Meet one-on-one to understand specific concerns, (2) involve them in requirements definition so they feel ownership, (3) demonstrate how the CRM solves specific sales team pain points they care about, (4) ask the supportive CEO to champion the project in executive meetings, (5) identify a quick win for the sales team in the first phase.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Sales VP as priority
Justifies based on power + resistance gap

questions.question: Explain the difference between the Power-Interest grid and the Salience model for stakeholder classification, and when you would use each.
questions.answer: The Power-Interest grid classifies stakeholders on two dimensions (power and interest) into four quadrants, resulting in four management strategies. It is simple and quick, best for projects with relatively few stakeholders where a broad categorization is sufficient. The Salience model classifies stakeholders on three dimensions (power, legitimacy, urgency) into seven categories, from dormant (power only) to definitive (all three). It is more nuanced, best for complex projects with many stakeholders where understanding the TYPE of influence matters. Use the Power-Interest grid for smaller projects or initial triage, and the Salience model for complex stakeholder environments requiring more sophisticated management.

rubric.key: Appropriate guidance on when to use each

quizzes.topic: Managing Resistance and Complex Stakeholder Environments

questions.question: Using the SCARF model, analyze why middle managers often resist organizational change projects and recommend one strategy addressing each threatened SCARF element.
questions.answer: Status: change may reduce their authority or make their expertise obsolete. Strategy: involve them as change agents, giving them visible roles. Certainty: they don't know how the change affects their jobs. Strategy: communicate a clear timeline and what specifically changes for them. Autonomy: decisions are being made for them, not with them. Strategy: give them control over HOW to implement the change in their departments. Relatedness: their team dynamics and reporting relationships may change. Strategy: preserve existing teams where possible and create opportunities for new relationships. Fairness: they may feel targeted or treated differently than other groups. Strategy: apply consistent change processes and communicate the rationale transparently.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies threats to at least 4 SCARF elements
Provides specific strategy for each element

questions.question: Describe two techniques for managing stakeholders who have conflicting requirements, and explain when each technique is most appropriate.
questions.answer: Technique 1: Weighted scoring matrix - assign weights to business criteria (strategic alignment, revenue impact, user base size), have stakeholders score options against criteria, select based on highest weighted score. Most appropriate when conflicts are about priority/sequence and objective data is available. Technique 2: Facilitated negotiation workshop - bring conflicting stakeholders together, focus on underlying interests rather than positions, generate creative options that address multiple interests. Most appropriate when conflicts are about approach or design and compromise solutions may exist that satisfy both parties' core needs.

rubric.key: Explains when each is most appropriate

major_assignments.title: Stakeholder Management Strategy for a Complex Project
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive stakeholder management strategy for a complex project with at least 20 stakeholders, including identification, analysis, engagement planning, and resistance management.
objectives: Identify and classify 20+ stakeholders using multiple models
Create an engagement assessment matrix with gap analysis
Develop targeted strategies for key stakeholders including resistant ones
Design monitoring mechanisms to track engagement effectiveness
deliverables: Power-Interest grid and Salience model analysis
Engagement assessment matrix with current vs. desired levels
Detailed engagement strategies for top 10 stakeholders (1 page each)

criteria: 20+ stakeholders identified across internal and external groups
Both Power-Interest and Salience models correctly applied
Analysis is thorough and demonstrates deep understanding
Current vs. desired levels accurately assessed for all stakeholders
Strategies are specific to each stakeholder, not generic
Plans address timing and phase-specific engagement needs
Resistant stakeholders identified with root cause analysis
Conversion strategies use SCARF or similar frameworks
Dashboard includes leading and lagging indicators
Feedback loops built into the monitoring process

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on stakeholder analysis, engagement, and resistance management

midterm_examination.description: Stakeholder identification, analysis, and engagement planning

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including complex environments and monitoring

class_participation.description: Participation in role-plays, case studies, and stakeholder analysis exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Role-play sessions require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Case study discussions are collaborative. The stakeholder strategy assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all frameworks and models referenced.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to spreadsheet and presentation software. Stakeholder analysis tools (optional) enhance the assignment.
